extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is set true only by r1, whose antecedent requires all of {peptic_ulcer, hepatic_impairment, elderly, heart_failure, prior_gi_bleed, immunosuppressed, polypharmacy, bradycardia} = true. rec_drug_b is set true by r2 whenever peptic_ulcer = true. Since r1's antecedent includes peptic_ulcer = true, any input that fires r1 also necessarily fires r2. The two rules target different outputs (rec_drug_a vs rec_drug_b), so priorities and tie-breaking are irrelevant, and the all-false default never applies to an output a rule has set. Therefore the antecedent of r1 forces both rec_drug_a and rec_drug_b to true, violating the mutual-exclusion property (rec_drug_a and rec_drug_b never jointly true). Concrete counterexample: set the eight r1-antecedent inputs to true (remaining inputs false); r1 yields rec_drug_a=true and r2 yields rec_drug_b=true.
